With Japan maintaining one of the world’s highest credit card penetration rates, the introduction of Bitcoin-settled credit products could meaningfully expand crypto’s role in everyday transactions, particularly among existing BTC holders seeking frictionless spending mechanisms without exiting their positions.

data.title: Tether CEO: Building Open Source Infrastructure to Achieve Financial Inclusion
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino delivered a speech at the 2026 Bitcoin Conference in Las Vegas. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino introduced Tether's newly released The Resilience Stack, which integrates its peer-to-peer communication protocol HolePunch, decentralized communication application Keet, open-source self-hosted wallet toolkit WDK, and local AI development platform QVAC into an infrastructure system for people without access to basic financial and communication services. Keet was previously not open-source, but Ardoino formally pledged to promote its open-source development, and related documentation and modules are currently being prepared. Furthermore, Paolo Ardoino stated that Tether has launched the BTC Faucet; users who download the Tether wallet application can instantly receive a small amount of Bitcoin via the Lightning Network by replying to the Tether Wallet tweet as required. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino began with a core metaphor from Isaac Asimov's psychohistorical theory in his Foundation series, explaining that Tether's fundamental mission is not merely to issue the USDT stablecoin and accumulate Bitcoin in the long term, but also to address the "global dark crisis" of social unrest, systemic turmoil, inflation, financial exclusion, and power monopolies. He argued that Tether aims to enhance social resilience and bridge the development gap by building a long-term technological system. Ardoino pointed out that multiple real crises have already emerged globally: hundreds of millions of people face unstable electricity supply, large populations are excluded from the traditional financial system, and large corporations and institutions monopolize technology and unilaterally exploit users, leading to a slow and continuous destabilization of society. Simultaneously, nearly half the population lacks access to basic financial services, and the rapid development of AI technology will further amplify the global wealth gap and digital literacy disparity, exacerbating social divisions. Paolo Ardoino stated that the core of combating systemic decay is not short-term resistance, but rather building a long-term, decentralized, and censorship-resistant underlying infrastructure. This is also the core motivation behind Tether's creation of The Resilience Stack. Paolo Ardoino explained that around The Resilience Stack, Tether has launched a full-chain open-source technology ecosystem, constructing a decentralized system layer by layer: the bottom layer is the HolePunch peer-to-peer communication protocol, which is serverless, censorship-resistant, and highly scalable, reshaping decentralized network transmission capabilities; on top of this is the censorship-free instant messaging application Keet, enabling secure and private global communication, which will be fully open-sourced in the future; relying on the open-source wallet library WDK, it promotes self-custodial wallets, adapting to the peer-to-peer Bitcoin payment needs of ordinary people, smart devices, and AI Agnet, providing solutions for high-frequency micro-transactions in the era of the Internet of Things; the top layer implements the QVAC local decentralized AI tool, ensuring user data privacy, adapting to low-end devices and remote areas, and practicing the philosophy that "AI that doesn't belong to you isn't your intelligence." In addition, Paolo Ardoino stated that Tether boasts a massive global user base, covering 160 countries and over 573 million users, with its ecosystem experiencing rapid growth. It also continues to invest heavily in open-source development, having launched over a thousand open-source projects. Tether plans to attract global developers to co-build its ecosystem through funding, hackathons, and other means. In his speech, Paolo Ardoino emphasized that Tether's long-term vision is to break down corporate and institutional monopolies by leveraging an integrated open-source technology stack encompassing communications, finance, and decentralized AI. This will achieve financial inclusion, freedom of communication, and controllable privacy, relying on the public and decentralized technology to build a stable order, mitigate potential global risks, and complete long-term societal repair and innovation. The following is the full text of Paolo Ardoino's speech, compiled by Jinse Finance (with Deepseek assistance). I'm very happy to be here again this year. Why am I showing this video? I want to explain as best as I can how we think about Tether. Tether is well-known as a stablecoin company, issuing USDT, and is a buyer of Bitcoin. We hold over 130,000 to 140,000 Bitcoins. We're constantly buying. Just today, we launched a Bitcoin faucet. If you're on Twitter, have a Tether BTC account, and you can do this using your Tether.me email address (which you'll get when you download your Tether wallet), you'll receive 10 cents of Bitcoin directly into your wallet for free. Yes, we do all these things, but I want to explain to you why Tether is so much more than that. The best way I can think of to draw inspiration is from Isaac Asimov, my all-time favorite author. If you've read Asimov's Foundation series, there's a character, a mathematician named Hari Seldon, who created a theory called psychohistory.

Psychohistory is a theory, a combination of mathematics, statistics, physics, sociology, and political science; it is a complete science used to understand how humanity and society will transform, shape, and change over hundreds, thousands, or millions of years. This is an excerpt from the book. We use this to express—as Asimov very eloquently puts it in the book—that you can use science to predict and analyze what is happening in the universe, predict the outcome, and while predicting the outcome, try to think about how to use the same science to adjust it, push things in a different direction, change the course of social decay, and change the potential darkness that may befall us all. Just as in the book, Asimov describes this Hari Seldon prediction that after the Empire (the most powerful empire in the galaxy) has existed for ten thousand years, something will happen, a turmoil will occur, followed by ten thousand years of darkness. He also predicts that, by using psychohistory, the darkness can be shortened to only a few hundred years.

Why am I telling you this? Why did I bring up these apocalyptic discussions at the Bitcoin conference?

I think **my way of thinking about Bitcoin is that it's a technology, the beginning of a new social structure, creating a new spark. This spark will survive and resist any darkness because it's designed to be peer-to-peer. It's created by people, by the people who use it, by the people in every corner of the world who accept it.** So, if we believe this is indeed true, if we believe Bitcoin is indeed the first element in the potential struggle against the coming darkness, what can we learn from it? First, when I think about what Asimov was trying to convey through his psychohistorical science, I wonder, is this just science fiction? Does it only happen in books? Then I think that the darkness described in Asimov's books can actually be reduced to and brought back to Earth, because the darkness described in those books is nothing more than social chaos, social instability. You may have heard me mention last year that Tether, to us—we call it the "stability company" internally—is a company that aims to bring stability to society. Hari Seldon, in Asimov's books, attempted to solve and reduce the darkness from ten thousand years to a few hundred years through the science of psychohistory. And in Tether, we believe the darkness that is coming in the societies we are seeing—it's there. We see war, we see inflation, we see our currencies being destroyed, we see instability, we see all the signs that what we call the coming darkness—the world isn't necessarily heading for a better place; there's more chaos, more instability. So I wanted to use this video to explain how, starting with the universe and science fiction, we can actually reduce it to what's happening on Earth. Let me move on to the next part. This is a story that not only predicts the future but also identifies the present. Right now, 700 million people are living on intermittent power. They are already in the dark. Families that have farmed for generations to support their communities are abandoned overnight because the system deems them economically unviable. In one of the oldest democracies on Earth, people are arrested not for violence, but for their speech, for an emoji. The lessons of some governments are clear: they don't need to silence us; they just need to make sure we can't afford our rent. In the most powerful countries, corporations are building machines not to serve the people, but to exploit them. The darkness doesn't come with an explosion, but with a slow, gradual darkening. So, let's return to Earth from space science fiction. The decades of darkness in the universe described in the book translates to a more unstable and unpredictable society on Earth. Hundreds of millions—in fact, billions—lack access to basic financial services. They lack electricity and reliable telecommunications. More importantly, they lack, and will not have access to, basic smart services. Consider that four billion people worldwide lack access to basic financial services. They are left behind by the traditional financial system. How can we assume that excluding half the world's population will bring more stability to society? This is the analogy we're trying to draw from the revelations of psychohistory: if we do nothing about it, if we don't do something, if we don't try to use science, technology, and our abilities to build something different—something that lasts longer than darkness, something that reduces darkness by creating stability, by creating points of light in nations and cities, in people, something that connects people (wherever they are) and makes them resilient to change—that will be the darkness on our planet. But you know what all this talk means? What does Tether intend to do about it? Let's read on. Hari Seldon understands what most people miss: you don't fight darkness by winning a battle, you fight it by building a system that can outlast darkness. That's what we're doing. A foundation, real infrastructure, we call "The Resilience Stack." That's our answer. The Resilience Stack is an open-source technology stack, a scientific approach, like a case of psychohistory, analyzing the different problems we see in all the countries we operate in. Remember, Tether operates in hundreds of countries, in 160 different countries. We have people on the ground. We talk to people. We have a network of 573 million users using USDT, Tether Gold, and all the other services we offer, and that network is growing. Our network is growing by 34 million new wallets every quarter. That's unprecedented. This scale proves that the technology we're building is growing at the speed of a social network. It's no longer just a fintech company, no longer just a stablecoin; it's becoming a movement, a technology stack that's becoming part of the fabric of the world—a world where parents are left behind by the traditional financial system, left behind by the ability to communicate securely with their children, and who may not even have electricity in their homes. Imagine a world where half the world's population lacks access to basic financial services and doesn't participate in the 100-fold increase in intelligence capabilities brought about by artificial intelligence. If the gap between the two sides of society is already so large due to financial inclusion, imagine what will happen when AI truly becomes a part of everyone's life. This gap, which is currently dividing two large halves of the world's population, will widen a hundredfold because of AI. The Resilience Stack is our response to this problem. What are the concrete, tangible things? What are the actual technologies, like those in psychohistory, that we can use, build, and apply to reduce dark ages from centuries or decades to just a few years and create those sparks throughout society? In this way, no matter what happens, no matter how dystopian the future, no matter what disaster, no matter what pandemic, no matter what, we can still connect. We can transact together with Bitcoin. We can use AI services that serve us, not just a few large corporations. All of this comes together in a single story, a single stack. You should look at Tether's GitHub, the open-source code repository. I think we've crossed 1000 open-source projects on GitHub. That's quite unique. It shows that we really care deeply about building something that lasts longer than our company, because software technology needs to be resilient to its creators as well. That's also one of the things I love about Bitcoin: it lasts longer than its creators, it will last forever. Of course, we will always remember Satoshi Nakamoto. We are all Satoshi Nakamoto. But that's the beauty of technology. It means that when a technology is done well, everyone can use it, no matter who created it. Everyone is actually a father of that technology. Let's look at the very bottom, the first part. A peer-to-peer protocol, no servers, no central authority to maintain it. We've built **HolePunch**, a fully peer-to-peer telecommunications protocol that scales to billions of users, billions of machines, and trillions of AI agents. For the more tech-savvy among you, it's like the BitTorrent protocol built about 30 years ago, rewritten from scratch, improved, with added encryption layers, making it incredibly scalable and adaptable for not only file sharing but also any real-time data streaming for video calls, messaging, mapping, and much more. It's completely open source. Everyone should check it out because the HolePunch protocol is the first to allow any developer to build applications that scale to billions of people and hundreds of thousands of companies without any single central server. Building on that, we've proven—and we'll see later—that we ourselves have the capability to provide a truly scalable service to all of humanity without any single point of failure. You can build any application on top of it. Similarly, you can build a peer-to-peer Uber, a peer-to-peer mapping system, anything you want. It's free and one of the most sophisticated yet easy-to-use software and networking stacks, empowering in incredible ways anything you build for your company, yourself, your family, or your friends. In my view, this is like Bitcoin to finance. HolePunch to the internet and telecommunications is like Bitcoin to finance. On top of that, there is communication that cannot be suppressed or shut down.

On top of the HolePunch protocol and stack, we built Keet. I'm not sure how many of you have used Keet. It's an example of a messaging application with over 5 million users on desktop and mobile, probably more, because it's hard to count because there's no central server. There are millions of users and chat rooms with tens of thousands of users sharing tens of millions of messages, videos, photos, and so on, without any central server. Keet is the first messaging application that can scale to 8 billion people and eventually to machines, at no cost because it doesn't have any data centers. It cannot be blocked. It works everywhere, even under the strictest, most stringent types of internet control. We built this for people. We built this for fathers who want a reliable and secure way to talk to their children, but also for people working and living in potentially dystopian or authoritarian regimes. This is truly the first unstoppable communication application. I want to say one thing. One of the main criticisms of the Keet application has always been: why not open source? I can assure you, I am committed to making it open source. We are working on all the documentation, we are working on the individual modules, so that once it's open source, everyone can take it and improve it, build on it, change it, and recreate it in a single, simple step. Here's an example. This is a huge amount of work the Tether team has done to open source it, and we are doing it from the bottom of our hearts because we believe that if people can't talk to each other, if people can't send messages to each other, if we block the ability to communicate peer-to-peer—just like society was born from peer-to-peer communication. We meet on the street, we meet in the city square, we talk to each other without any intermediaries. For the past 50 years, intermediaries have taken over the financial world and the communications world. That's why society is in its darkest hour, because instead of using technology to create a more open society, we have hijacked technology, making society tightly controlled by a few companies. So Keet, and Keet's open source, will be one of the most important moments for us, one of the greatest testaments to our dedication to this mission. Then there are financial instruments, powered by WDK, serving humans, machines, and the agents between them to integrate unstoppable Bitcoin payments. WDK is one of our most successful products; it's an open-source library that allows any developer, anyone, any computer, and any AI agent to have a self-custodied wallet. We believe that in the future, billions of people, billions of machines, and trillions of AI agents will need a self-custodied wallet. People need control over their wealth. People need to be able to transact with anyone they want. So we wanted to create something that everyone could use, supporting any asset, but most importantly, supporting Bitcoin. From a purely physics perspective—technology is physics—from a purely technophysics perspective, we know the best way to scale future payment demands is when trillions of payments will be needed every day as AI agents become ubiquitous, and current financial transmission layers will be unable to handle it. Something like the Lightning Network is the right direction because it's Bitcoin's peer-to-peer transaction layer. I'm excited to see WDK supporting this from the very beginning. We want to ensure that your smart refrigerator, smart car, and smartphone can be transacted with Bitcoin, while protecting your Bitcoin and ensuring it always belongs to you. At the very top is decentralized artificial intelligence. Because if it's not your AI, it's not your intelligence. The final part is QVAC. We just released the QVAC SDK, an open-source software development kit that allows anyone to build AI tools that run locally on your smartphone, your device, and your laptop, thus guaranteeing your privacy. It supports all the best open-source large language models. It scales from the smallest GPU you own to your laptop, smartphone, all the way to mainframes. It embeds the HolePunch protocol, it embeds WDK, it embeds everything we've seen before. Because we drew inspiration from "If it's not your key, it's not your coin"—and we all know what that means for Bitcoin—we replicated the same concept, we understood: if it's not your AI, it's not your intelligence. We're driven—these days, these weeks, these years—we've been talking about AI. We know it's one of the most fundamentally transformative technologies for the future of society and humanity. But let me reiterate, if we don't build AI tools that cover the needs of that segment of the population—that half of humanity without access to basic financial services—if we pretend that those without access to basic financial services are also those who can't afford OpenAI or Anthropic subscriptions, these people cannot be left behind. They need tools that can work on their small smartphones, tools that can work in the most remote and smallest villages in Africa, Central South America, or Southeast Asia. We want Tether to build a coherent story, from telecommunications to messaging, to wallets and self-custodial wallets, to AI, creating a complete, unique, and fully open-source stack that empowers people, not companies, not enterprises, but simply people. Because ultimately, it comes back to science fiction: people will rebuild the universe, people will rebuild society, or rather, they will save society on Earth. People will make society on Earth more stable, without looking at light-years away from our solar system. So I suggest and recommend that each of you take a look at what we are building. Contribute, and again, it's all completely open source, on the Tether website. We will soon release funding so everyone can contribute to this technology and build upon it. We will be hosting a hackathon, and we will do everything we can to help people own what we build, be proud of their contributions, and ensure this technology remains resilient even in the face of God's wrath. Thank you very much. Have a great conference.
